question.text: In mutual funds, what is a Switch transaction?

answer.text: Changing from Direct Plan to Regular Plan
Redeeming units from one scheme and investing the proceeds in another scheme of the same fund house
Changing the bank account linked to the mutual fund folio

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A Switch transaction involves simultaneously redeeming units from one scheme (source scheme) and purchasing units in another scheme (target scheme) within the same fund house. It is essentially a redemption and a purchase done in one transaction. Switch can also be done between plans (Direct to Regular or vice versa) or options (Growth to IDCW or vice versa) of the same scheme. **The Trap:** Switch is treated as a redemption from the source scheme for tax purposes. Capital gains tax applies on the redeemed units as if they were sold. The purchase in the target scheme is treated as a fresh investment. **Cross-Exam Context:** Also tested in V-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Before recommending a switch to a retired investor, always consider the tax implications. If they have significant unrealized gains in the source scheme, switching will trigger capital gains tax. Sometimes it may be better to keep existing investments and invest fresh money in the desired scheme.
